name: TypeScript Deep Cuts
description: Go beyond basics ‚ÄĒ generics, utility types, type guards, conditional types, and real-world TS patterns.

cards.front: What are Generics in TypeScript, why use them, and give a real example?
Explain Partial, Required, Pick, and Omit utility types with examples.
cards.back:
These transform existing types without rewriting them:

‚ÄĘ `Partial<T>` ‚ÄĒ makes ALL props optional
  ‚Üí Great for update/patch functions
  `Partial<User>` ‚Üí { name?: string; age?: number }

‚ÄĘ `Required<T>` ‚ÄĒ makes ALL props required (opposite of Partial)
  ‚Üí Useful to enforce completeness at boundaries

‚ÄĘ `Pick<T, Keys>` ‚ÄĒ grabs only the listed keys
  `Pick<User, 'name' | 'email'>` ‚Üí just those two fields

‚ÄĘ `Omit<T, Keys>` ‚ÄĒ removes the listed keys
  `Omit<User, 'password'>` ‚Üí everything except password

‚ÄĘ Combine them: `Partial<Pick<User, 'bio' | 'avatar'>>`

cards.front: Explain Record, Exclude, Extract, and ReturnType utility types.
cards.back:
‚ÄĘ `Record<Keys, Value>` ‚ÄĒ builds an object type from key/value types
  `Record<string, number>` ‚Üí { [k: string]: number }
  `Record<'admin' | 'user', Permission[]>` ‚Üí exact key map

‚ÄĘ `Exclude<Union, Removed>` ‚ÄĒ removes members from a union
  `Exclude<'a' | 'b' | 'c', 'a'>` ‚Üí 'b' | 'c'

‚ÄĘ `Extract<Union, Kept>` ‚ÄĒ keeps only matching members
  `Extract<string | number | boolean, string>` ‚Üí string

‚ÄĘ `ReturnType<Fn>` ‚ÄĒ extracts the return type of a function
  `ReturnType<typeof fetchUser>` ‚Üí Promise<User>
  ‚Üí Huge for typing without importing internal types

cards.front: What are type guards in TypeScript? Explain typeof, instanceof, and custom `is` guards.
cards.back:
Type guards narrow a type inside a conditional block so TS knows exactly what you have.

‚ÄĘ `typeof` ‚ÄĒ works for primitives
  `if (typeof x === 'string')` ‚Üí x is string inside block

‚ÄĘ `instanceof` ‚ÄĒ works for classes
  `if (err instanceof HttpError)` ‚Üí access HttpError props

‚ÄĘ Custom `is` guard ‚ÄĒ a function returning `param is Type`
```
function isUser(val: unknown): val is User {
  return typeof val === 'object' && val !== null && 'name' in val;
}
```
  ‚Üí TS trusts YOUR logic to narrow the type
  ‚Üí Essential for API responses, JSON parsing, form data

‚ÄĘ Without guards, you'd cast with `as` everywhere (unsafe!)

cards.front: What's the difference between `unknown` and `any`? When should you use each?
cards.back:
Both accept any value, but they differ in what you can DO with them:

‚ÄĘ `any` ‚ÄĒ disables ALL type checking
  ‚Üí You can call methods, access props, pass it anywhere
  ‚Üí Basically opts out of TypeScript
  ‚Üí Use only for rapid prototyping or legacy migration

‚ÄĘ `unknown` ‚ÄĒ the type-safe top type
  ‚Üí You MUST narrow before using (via type guard, typeof, etc.)
  ‚Üí Forces you to prove what you have before acting

Rule of thumb:
‚ÄĘ Receiving data from outside? ‚Üí `unknown`
‚ÄĘ Migrating JS code quickly? ‚Üí `any` (temporarily)
‚ÄĘ In library APIs? ‚Üí almost always `unknown`

`unknown` = safe any. Prefer it in 99% of cases.

cards.front: What are discriminated unions (tagged unions) in TypeScript?
How do conditional types work in TypeScript? (T extends U ? X : Y)
cards.back:
Conditional types let you pick a type based on a condition ‚ÄĒ like a ternary but for types.

```
type IsString<T> = T extends string ? true : false;
IsString<'hi'>   // true
IsString<42>     // false
```

Key behaviors:
‚ÄĘ Distributes over unions by default:
  `IsString<string | number>` ‚Üí true | false (checks each member)

‚ÄĘ Used heavily in utility type internals:
  `Exclude<T, U> = T extends U ? never : T`

‚ÄĘ Combine with `infer` for type extraction (see tsa-009)

‚ÄĘ Wrap in `[T] extends [U]` to prevent distribution

‚ÄĘ Real use: conditional return types, API type routing, feature flags at type level

cards.front: What are template literal types in TypeScript?
cards.back:
They let you build string types from other types using backtick syntax ‚ÄĒ like template literals but at the type level.

```
type Color = 'red' | 'blue';
type Size = 'sm' | 'lg';
type Token = `${Color}-${Size}`;
// 'red-sm' | 'red-lg' | 'blue-sm' | 'blue-lg'
```

‚ÄĘ TS generates all permutations automatically
‚ÄĘ Built-in helpers: `Uppercase<T>`, `Lowercase<T>`, `Capitalize<T>`, `Uncapitalize<T>`

Real uses:
‚ÄĘ CSS class name types: `bg-${Color}`
‚ÄĘ Event handler keys: `on${Capitalize<EventName>}`
‚ÄĘ Route patterns: `/api/${Resource}/${string}`
‚ÄĘ Enforce naming conventions at the type level

cards.front: What does the `infer` keyword do in conditional types?
cards.back:
`infer` lets you extract a type from inside another type during a conditional check ‚ÄĒ like pattern matching.

```
type UnwrapPromise<T> =
  T extends Promise<infer U> ? U : T;

UnwrapPromise<Promise<string>> // string
UnwrapPromise<number>          // number
```

‚ÄĘ `infer U` declares a type variable U that TS fills in by matching the structure
‚ÄĘ Only works inside the `extends` clause of a conditional type

More examples:
‚ÄĘ Get function args: `T extends (...args: infer A) => any ? A : never`
‚ÄĘ Get array element: `T extends (infer E)[] ? E : T`
‚ÄĘ Get return type: `T extends (...args: any) => infer R ? R : never`
  ‚Üí This is literally how `ReturnType<T>` works internally

cards.front: What are mapped types in TypeScript?
cards.back:
Mapped types iterate over keys to transform an existing type into a new one.

```
type Readonly<T> = {
  readonly [K in keyof T]: T[K];
};
```

Anatomy: `[K in Keys]: NewValueType`

‚ÄĘ `keyof T` gets all property names as a union
‚ÄĘ Add/remove modifiers with `+` / `-`:
  `-readonly [K in keyof T]` ‚Üí makes all props mutable
  `[K in keyof T]-?` ‚Üí makes all props required

‚ÄĘ Remap keys with `as`:
  `[K in keyof T as `get${Capitalize<K>}`]: () => T[K]`
  ‚Üí turns `{ name: string }` into `{ getName: () => string }`

‚ÄĘ `Partial`, `Required`, `Readonly`, `Pick` are all mapped types under the hood

cards.front: What does `as const` do in TypeScript?
Enums vs union types in TypeScript ‚ÄĒ what are the tradeoffs?
cards.back:
Both model a fixed set of values, but they behave differently:

‚ÄĘ String Enums:
  ‚Üí Exist at runtime (generate JS code)
  ‚Üí Support reverse mapping (numeric only)
  ‚Üí Can't be tree-shaken easily
  ‚Üí Namespace isolation: `Status.Active`

‚ÄĘ Union Types (`type Status = 'active' | 'inactive'`):
  ‚Üí Zero runtime cost ‚ÄĒ erased during compile
  ‚Üí Fully tree-shakeable
  ‚Üí Work naturally with `as const` objects
  ‚Üí Better with discriminated unions

‚ÄĘ `const enum` ‚ÄĒ inlined at compile time but has edge cases with declaration files

Senior recommendation:
‚Üí Prefer union types + `as const` for most cases
‚Üí Use enums only when you need runtime iteration or interop with backend enum values

cards.front: What is declaration merging and module augmentation in TypeScript?
What does the `satisfies` operator do in TypeScript?
cards.back:
`satisfies` validates that a value matches a type WITHOUT widening it ‚ÄĒ you keep the narrow inferred type.

```
type Colors = Record<string, string | string[]>;

const palette = {
  red: '#ff0000',
  gray: ['#ccc', '#999'],
} satisfies Colors;

palette.red.toUpperCase();   // ‚úÖ TS knows it's string
palette.gray.map(g => g);    // ‚úÖ TS knows it's string[]
```

Without `satisfies` (using `: Colors` instead):
‚Üí `palette.red` would be `string | string[]` ‚ÄĒ you'd lose the narrowing

When to use:
‚ÄĘ Config objects ‚ÄĒ validate shape, keep literal types
‚ÄĘ Theme tokens, route maps, lookup tables
‚ÄĘ Anywhere you want type-checking + autocomplete on the exact value

Added in TypeScript 4.9

cards.front: What are the key strict mode flags in tsconfig and what do they catch?
cards.back:
`"strict": true` enables ALL strict flags at once. Key individual ones:

‚ÄĘ `strictNullChecks` ‚ÄĒ `null`/`undefined` aren't assignable to other types
  ‚Üí Forces you to handle null cases explicitly

‚ÄĘ `noImplicitAny` ‚ÄĒ errors when TS can't infer a type and would default to `any`
  ‚Üí Forces explicit typing on ambiguous params

‚ÄĘ `strictFunctionTypes` ‚ÄĒ enforces contravariant function parameter checks
  ‚Üí Catches subtle callback type bugs

‚ÄĘ `strictPropertyInitialization` ‚ÄĒ class props must be set in constructor or declared optional

‚ÄĘ `noUncheckedIndexedAccess` (not in strict, but highly recommended)
  ‚Üí `arr[0]` returns `T | undefined` instead of just `T`

‚ÄĘ `exactOptionalPropertyTypes` ‚ÄĒ distinguishes `undefined` from missing

Senior tip: Always start with `"strict": true` and add `noUncheckedIndexedAccess` for maximum safety.
